posts.title: Agents that work vs agents that demo
posts.hook: Most AI agents I see demoed on stage would break in 30 seconds on real production data.
posts.body:
Most AI agents I see demoed on stage would break in 30 seconds on real production data.

I've been building agentic workflows for GTM ops since early 2025. Clay enrichments, automated scoring, CRM writes, the whole pipeline. Here's the pattern I keep seeing.

Teams build an agent that works on 10 clean records. They demo it. Everyone claps. Then they run it on 10,000 real records and it falls apart.

Missing fields. Malformed URLs. Companies that merged. People who changed jobs. Data that doesn't match the schema the agent was trained on.

The gap between demo agent and production agent is entirely about error handling. Not the cool parts. The boring parts.

What actually works in production:

✅ Validate every input before the agent touches it
✅ Build fallback paths for every enrichment step
✅ Log everything so you can debug at 2am when the pipeline breaks
✅ Run on a small batch first, compare output to manual work, then scale

The frameworks are getting better. LangChain, CrewAI, plain Claude tool use. But the framework isn't the bottleneck. Your data quality is.

Stop building agents that impress. Start building agents that survive Tuesday morning when your CRM has 47 records with "N/A" in the domain field.
posts.cta: What's the ugliest edge case that broke your automation?

remixes.label: failure-autopsy
remixes.hook: I killed a production pipeline last month. An agent I built corrupted 400 CRM records in 90 seconds.
remixes.body:
I killed a production pipeline last month. An agent I built corrupted 400 CRM records in 90 seconds.

The agent worked perfectly in staging. Clean data, predictable schemas, every test green. Then it hit production and encountered a company name with a comma in it. That one comma cascaded through the CSV parser, shifted every column, and the agent started writing revenue figures into the email field.

Here's what I learned rebuilding it.

First, synthetic test data is a lie. It's too clean. Your agent needs to train on the ugliest exports your ops team actually produces. Ask for the spreadsheet that made someone cry last quarter. Use that.

Second, agents need circuit breakers, not just error handling. My rebuilt pipeline now halts if more than 2% of records throw exceptions in any batch. It doesn't try to be clever about recovery. It stops and alerts me.

Third, the gap between demo and production isn't about scale. It's about entropy. Real data has merged duplicates, half-migrated fields, and values from three different CRMs stitched together over five years.

The agents that survive production aren't smarter. They're more paranoid. They validate at every step. They assume the data is lying to them.

Because usually, it is.
remixes.cta: What's the worst data quality issue that took down one of your automations?

remixes.label: build-checklist
remixes.hook: Before I deploy any AI agent to production, it has to pass five tests. Most fail on test two.
remixes.body:
Before I deploy any AI agent to production, it has to pass five tests. Most fail on test two.

Test 1: Does it handle nulls without hallucinating defaults? If a field is empty, the agent should flag it, not invent a value. I've seen agents fabricate phone numbers from company names. That's not enrichment. That's fiction.

Test 2: Can it process 1,000 records without drifting from its instructions? This is where most agents break. They follow the prompt perfectly for 50 records, then start improvising. Context window fatigue is real.

Test 3: Does it fail loudly? Silent failures are the worst kind. If the agent can't complete a task, I need a log entry, not a blank field I discover three weeks later.

Test 4: Can a non-technical person understand what it did? If I can't show my output to an ops lead and have them verify it in 60 seconds, the agent is a black box. Black boxes don't survive in GTM.

Test 5: Does it cost less than the human process it replaces? Not theoretically. Actually. With real token counts on real data.

I run these five checks on every agent before it touches production. The ones that pass all five are boring. Predictable. Reliable.

That's the point.
remixes.cta: What's on your pre-deployment checklist for AI agents?

tones.advisor:
Most AI agents I see demoed on stage would break in 30 seconds on real production data.

I've been deploying agentic workflows in GTM operations since early 2025, and I consistently see the same failure pattern. Here's what I recommend teams internalize before investing in agent infrastructure.

The demo environment is not your environment. Clean records, consistent schemas, and predictable inputs create a false confidence that collapses on contact with real data. My recommendation: before any production deployment, run your agent against your messiest historical export. The one with merged duplicates, missing fields, and three naming conventions.

Build for failure volume, not failure type. You cannot predict every edge case. What you can do is set thresholds. If error rates exceed 2% in any batch, halt the pipeline. Don't build elaborate recovery logic. Build reliable stop mechanisms.

Invest in observability before capability. Logging every decision your agent makes costs almost nothing compared to debugging a silent failure three weeks after it corrupted your CRM. Every write operation should produce a traceable log.

The organizations getting value from AI agents aren't the ones with the most sophisticated models. They're the ones with the most disciplined deployment practices. Treat agents like junior employees. Trust but verify. Always verify.

And the people building them know it. That's the dirty secret of the agent hype cycle. The demo is the product. The production version is someone else's problem.

I've been shipping agentic workflows since early 2025. Clay enrichments, CRM writes, scoring pipelines. The stuff that actually touches revenue. And I can tell you that 90% of what gets applause at conferences is engineering theater.

Clean data in, clean data out. Wow. Groundbreaking. Now run it on the spreadsheet your sales ops lead exported from the CRM you migrated from two years ago, the one where half the records have commas in company names and the phone number field contains LinkedIn URLs.

Watch what happens.

The agent community has a demo addiction. We celebrate prototypes and ignore production. We share screen recordings of perfect runs and quietly delete the ones where the agent wrote revenue data into the email field.

Here's . Building an agent that works on 10 records is a weekend project. Building one that works on 10,000 records without supervision is a quarter of engineering work. And most teams aren't willing to do the boring part.

Stop clapping for demos. Start asking for production logs.

platforms.newsletter:
Something's been bugging me about the AI agent discourse.

Every week I see another demo on LinkedIn. Perfect data goes in, perfect output comes out, everyone's impressed. And every week I'm in my terminal watching an agent choke on a CSV where someone put a comma in a company name.

I've been building these pipelines for over a year now. The gap between what works in a demo and what works in production isn't about scale. It's about how messy real data actually is. Merged duplicates. Half-migrated fields. Phone numbers in the email column.

This week I want to break down the five things I check before any agent touches production data. None of them are exciting. All of them have saved me from corrupting a CRM.

posts.title: GPU costs are the new SaaS tax
posts.hook: We went from "software is eating the world" to "GPU compute is eating your budget."
posts.body:
We went from "software is eating the world" to "GPU compute is eating your budget."

I track my AI costs daily. Not because I'm cheap. Because the numbers are wild if you're not paying attention.

Last month I ran a content generation pipeline. Claude Opus for the heavy lifting, Sonnet for the high-frequency stuff, local Qwen for the cron jobs that run every hour. Total API spend was reasonable because I architected it that way.

But most teams don't. They throw Opus at everything. Summarization tasks that Haiku handles fine. Classification jobs that a local model crushes. Enrichment loops that could be cached but run fresh every time.

The infrastructure conversation Karpathy keeps raising isn't just about training clusters. It's about inference economics at scale.

Three things I've learned running AI in production daily:

1. Model routing saves 60-70% of costs. Match the model to the task complexity.
2. Cache aggressively. If you enriched that company yesterday, don't enrich it again today.
3. Local models for high-frequency, low-stakes work. My hourly crons run on Qwen and cost exactly zero.

The teams that win with AI in 2026 won't be the ones with the biggest GPU budget. They'll be the ones who treat compute like engineering, not magic.
posts.cta: How are you managing your AI inference costs? Curious what setups people are running.

remixes.label: model-routing-playbook
remixes.hook: I run three different AI models. Not because I love complexity. Because using one model for everything is like taking a helicopter to the grocery store.
remixes.body:
I run three different AI models. Not because I love complexity. Because using one model for everything is like taking a helicopter to the grocery store.

Here's my actual routing logic.

Claude Opus handles the tasks that need deep reasoning. Long-form content, complex code reviews, architecture decisions. These are the $15/million-token calls, and they're worth it when the output quality directly impacts revenue.

Sonnet handles the mid-tier stuff. Summarization, classification, shorter generation tasks. Good enough quality at a fraction of the cost. This covers maybe 60% of my daily API calls.

Local Qwen 2.5 handles the high-frequency cron jobs. The ones that run every hour. Status checks, light parsing, simple transformations. Zero API cost. Runs on my Mac Mini while I sleep.

The result: my total monthly AI spend is less than one SaaS subscription. And I'm running content pipelines, lead scoring, CRM enrichment, and analytics off of it.

Most people pick one model and throw it at everything. Then they're shocked when the bill arrives. Model routing isn't optimization. It's basic resource allocation. You wouldn't use the same tool for every job in any other context.

The question isn't which model is best. It's which model is best for this specific task at this specific volume.
remixes.cta: What's your model routing setup? Or are you still running everything through one model?

remixes.label: cost-tracking-wake-up
remixes.hook: I added one line of logging to my AI pipeline. It showed me I was spending $40/day on tasks a free local model could handle.
remixes.body:
I added one line of logging to my AI pipeline. It showed me I was spending $40/day on tasks a free local model could handle.

Here's what happened. I was running Claude Opus for everything. Content generation, data parsing, even simple JSON transformations in my cron jobs. The output quality was excellent across the board. But I wasn't looking at the token counts per task.

When I started logging input tokens, output tokens, and cost per call, the picture changed fast. 70% of my daily spend was going to tasks that required zero reasoning. Reformatting dates. Extracting fields from structured data. Generating short summaries of content I'd already written.

So I did an audit. Every task got classified: needs reasoning, needs good writing, or needs basic text processing. The basic processing moved to local Qwen. The good writing moved to Sonnet. Only the genuinely hard stuff stayed on Opus.

My monthly spend dropped by about 60%. Output quality stayed the same for the tasks that matter, because those tasks were still hitting the best model.

The problem isn't that AI is expensive. The problem is that most people have no idea what they're spending per task. They see a monthly total that seems fine, and never realize they're paying premium rates for work a calculator could do.

Track your costs per call. You'll be surprised.
remixes.cta: Do you know your cost per API call? Not your monthly total. Your per-task cost.

tones.advisor:
We went from "software is eating the world" to "GPU compute is eating your budget."

I track my AI costs daily, and I'd recommend the same for anyone running inference at scale. The numbers compound faster than most teams realize.

Here's the framework I use. Every AI task in my pipeline gets classified into one of three tiers.

Tier 1: High-reasoning tasks. Complex content generation, architectural decisions, nuanced analysis. These justify premium model costs. Claude Opus or equivalent. Worth the spend because output quality directly affects outcomes.

Tier 2: Mid-complexity tasks. Summarization, classification, shorter content. These should run on capable but cost-efficient models. Sonnet-class. The quality delta between Tier 1 and Tier 2 models is marginal for these use cases.

Tier 3: High-frequency, low-complexity tasks. Parsing, reformatting, simple extraction, cron jobs. Run these locally. Qwen, Llama, or similar. Zero API cost, and for these tasks, the output is indistinguishable.

My recommendation: before optimizing anything else, add per-call cost logging to your pipeline. Most teams discover that 60-70% of their API spend is on Tier 3 tasks hitting Tier 1 models. That's the lowest-hanging optimization available.

The organizations managing AI costs well aren't spending less. They're spending deliberately. Match the model to the task, track the cost per call, and audit monthly.
tones.provocateur:
We went from "software is eating the world" to "GPU compute is eating your budget."

And most of you have no idea how much you're burning. Because you're not tracking it. You're just vibing with the monthly invoice and hoping it's fine.

I track my AI costs daily. Here's what I found: 70% of my original spend was going to tasks that a free local model handles just as well. Date reformatting on Opus. JSON extraction on the most expensive model available. I was essentially paying a brain surgeon to take my temperature.

The AI industry loves this, by the way. They want you to throw everything at the biggest model. More tokens consumed, more revenue for them. Nobody at these companies is going to tell you that your cron job parsing HTML doesn't need a $15/million-token model.

So I stopped being lazy. Three-tier routing. Opus for the hard stuff. Sonnet for the medium stuff. Local Qwen for the hourly grind. My spend dropped 60%.

But here's the real provocation. Most teams will never do this. Because cost tracking isn't sexy. Model routing isn't a feature you can demo. And admitting you were wasting money means admitting you weren't paying attention.

GPU costs are the new SaaS tax, but only for people who refuse to read their own invoices.

posts.title: Outbound is broken because the data layer is broken
posts.hook: Your outbound isn't failing because of your copy. It's failing because you're emailing the wrong people at the wrong time.
posts.body:
Your outbound isn't failing because of your copy. It's failing because you're emailing the wrong people at the wrong time.

I spent years as an SDR sending 200+ emails a day. Primary domain. No warmup. SalesLoft sequences. Pure volume play. Some of those emails worked. Most didn't. And the ones that worked had nothing to do with my subject line.

They worked because the timing was right. The person had a problem. I happened to show up.

Now I build the systems that try to find those moments before the SDR ever hits send.

Intent signals from G2 and Bombora. Job postings that indicate tool evaluation. Technographic changes in BuiltWith. LinkedIn activity patterns. All of it piped through Clay, scored, and surfaced to the rep with context.

The automation isn't the hard part. Connecting Clay to your CRM to your sequencer takes an afternoon.

The hard part is building a signal model that actually predicts buying behavior instead of just generating noise. Most teams automate the wrong signals and wonder why reply rates tank.

Personalization without intent is just spam with someone's first name in it.

Fix your data layer. The copy will follow.
posts.cta: What intent signals are actually moving the needle for your outbound right now?

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Explains why outbound automation fails without personalized intent signals. Outlines a hybrid approach combining AI tools with human oversight for higher conversion rates. Provides case study from a SaaS company scaling outbound 3x.

remixes.label: intent-signal-hierarchy
remixes.hook: I ranked every intent signal I track by actual conversion rate. Job postings beat technographic data by 4x.
remixes.body:
I ranked every intent signal I track by actual conversion rate. Job postings beat technographic data by 4x.

After running outbound operations for years, first as an SDR doing manual volume, then building automated pipelines, I finally sat down and measured which signals actually predict a reply.

Here's the hierarchy from my data.

Tier 1: Hiring signals. When a company posts a role related to what you sell, they've already budget-approved the problem. This converts higher than anything else I track. Not close.

Tier 2: Tech stack changes. A company ripping out a competitor or adopting adjacent tools means they're actively reshaping that part of their operations. Timing matters here. You want to catch them mid-migration, not after.

Tier 3: Funding events. More budget unlocked, but it's noisy. A Series B doesn't tell you which department gets the money. Useful when combined with hiring signals. Useless alone.

Tier 4: Content engagement. Someone read your blog post or liked your LinkedIn post. Cool. That's awareness, not intent. Stop treating it like a buying signal.

The problem with most outbound isn't the copy or the cadence. It's that teams weight all signals equally and spray the same sequence at everyone. A job posting and a blog view should not trigger the same outreach.

Match your effort to the signal strength. The best copy in the world won't fix a bad target list.
remixes.cta: Which intent signal has the highest conversion rate in your pipeline?

remixes.label: sdrs-to-systems
remixes.hook: I used to send 200 cold emails a day by hand. Now a pipeline on my Mac Mini does better targeting than I ever did.
remixes.body:
I used to send 200 cold emails a day by hand. Now a pipeline on my Mac Mini does better targeting than I ever did.

When I was an SDR, outbound was a volume game. Load a list from ZoomInfo. Drop it into SalesLoft. Write five email variants. Hit send. Hope for the best. Some days I'd get replies. Most days I got silence or unsubscribes.

The emails that actually worked had one thing in common. I'd accidentally hit someone at the right moment. They were already evaluating solutions. My timing was lucky, not strategic.

Here's what changed when I started engineering the data layer instead of writing better copy.

First, I built enrichment pipelines that pull hiring data, tech stack changes, and funding events. Not manually. Automated, running daily.

Second, I built scoring that weights these signals. A company posting a relevant role scores higher than a company that just raised a round. Because hiring means they've committed budget to the problem.

Third, I stopped sending to anyone below a score threshold. Volume dropped by 80%. Reply rates tripled.

 for SDR teams: your reps aren't underperforming because of their email skills. They're underperforming because they're working off static lists with no real-time signal intelligence.

Fix the data layer first. The copy improvements can come later.
remixes.cta: Are your SDRs still working off static lists? What would change if the list updated itself daily?

Your outbound isn't failing because of your copy. It's failing because you're emailing the wrong people at the wrong time.

Having spent years in outbound, both as a practitioner and now building the systems behind it, here's what I'd recommend teams focus on.

Stop optimizing copy before fixing targeting. I've seen teams spend weeks A/B testing subject lines while their contact lists are six months stale. The highest-performing email I ever sent was poorly written. It worked because it hit a VP who'd posted a relevant job opening that week.

Invest in real-time signal infrastructure. Static lists decay at roughly 30% per year. By the time your SDR sends that email, the contact may have changed roles, the company may have already chosen a vendor, or the budget cycle may have closed. You need daily signal ingestion. Hiring data, tech stack changes, funding events.

Build a scoring model that reflects actual conversion. Not all signals are equal. In my data, a relevant job posting converts at 4x the rate of a funding event. Your scoring should reflect this hierarchy, not treat every signal the same.

Reduce volume, increase precision. The teams getting the best outbound results right now are sending fewer emails to better-targeted prospects. The volume play worked in 2019. The data layer play works now.

Fix your targeting infrastructure first. The copy refinements will compound on top of it.
tones.provocateur:
Your outbound isn't failing because of your copy. It's failing because you're emailing the wrong people at the wrong time.

And deep down, you already know this. You've A/B tested 47 subject lines. You've rewritten your cold email six times. You've tried humor, tried brevity, tried the "noticed you" opener. Reply rates haven't moved.

Because the problem was never the words. The problem is you're spraying a static list from three months ago and hoping someone happens to be in-market.

I spent years as an SDR doing exactly this. 200+ emails a day. Primary domain. No warmup. Pure volume. And the emails that worked? They worked because I got lucky on timing. Not because my copy was brilliant.

Here's what the outbound industry doesn't want to admit. Most SDR playbooks are built around compensating for bad data with more effort. Send more. Follow up more. Try more channels. The answer is always "more" because nobody wants to confront the fact that the targeting is broken.

Meanwhile, a scoring pipeline running on a Mac Mini that ingests hiring signals and tech stack changes daily will outperform a team of 10 SDRs working stale lists. Not because AI is magic. Because data freshness beats human effort when the effort is pointed in the wrong direction.

Stop writing better emails. Start building better lists.

posts.title: Cursor changed my build speed, not my thinking
posts.hook: AI coding tools made me mass 3x faster at building. They did not make me 3x better at deciding what to build.
posts.body:
AI coding tools made me 3x faster at building. They did not make me 3x better at deciding what to build.

I use Claude Code daily. Full context window on my monorepo. It writes components, debugs pipelines, scaffolds entire features. The velocity is real.

But I've also watched it confidently build the wrong thing. Twice last week I caught myself 45 minutes into an implementation that solved a problem I didn't actually have. The tool was fast. My specification was bad.

This is the thing nobody talks about with AI dev tools. They compress the build cycle so much that the bottleneck shifts entirely to taste and judgment.

Before: idea --> 2 weeks of building --> ship --> learn

remixes.label: specification-gap
remixes.hook: The bottleneck in AI-assisted development isn't the code. It's the spec you never wrote.
remixes.body:
The bottleneck in AI-assisted development isn't the code. It's the spec you never wrote.

I use Claude Code every day. It can scaffold a full feature in minutes. Build components, wire up API routes, write tests. The raw output speed is genuinely impressive.

But here's what happened three times last week. I gave the AI a vague prompt. It built something. I looked at it and realized it wasn't what I needed. Not because the code was bad. Because I hadn't thought clearly about what I actually wanted.

Before AI coding tools, the friction of writing code manually forced me to think through the problem. I'd plan because implementation was slow. Now implementation is fast, so I skip the planning. And I pay for it in rework.

My new workflow: I spend more time on specs than I ever did before. Not formal documents. Just clear, precise descriptions of what I want, why, and what the constraints are. A 10-minute spec saves an hour of AI-generated code that misses the point.

The irony of faster coding tools is that they increase the value of slower thinking. When implementation costs drop to near zero, the only thing that matters is whether you're building the right thing.

The developers who will thrive with AI tools aren't the ones who prompt the fastest. They're the ones who think the clearest before prompting.
remixes.cta: How has your planning process changed since you started using AI coding tools?

remixes.label: human-judgment-moat
remixes.hook: I let Claude Code make 100 decisions last week. It got 94 right. The 6 it got wrong would have cost me a week each.
remixes.body:
I let Claude Code make 100 decisions last week. It got 94 right. The 6 it got wrong would have cost me a week each.

That's the real math on AI coding tools. The accuracy is high enough to be dangerous. You start trusting the output. You review less carefully. You rubber-stamp generated code because it's been right 19 times in a row.

Then time 20 hits. The AI makes an architectural choice that seems reasonable in isolation but conflicts with a system constraint it doesn't know about. Or it picks a dependency that solves the immediate problem but creates a maintenance burden you'll carry for months.

Here are the 6 categories where I've learned to override AI suggestions.

Data model decisions. The AI optimizes for the current feature. I optimize for the next 10 features.

Dependency choices. The AI grabs the most popular library. I check maintenance status, bundle size, and whether we already have something that does the job.

API contract design. The AI builds what works now. I build what won't break when requirements shift.

Error handling boundaries. The AI catches everything locally. I decide what should propagate up.

Performance tradeoffs. The AI writes readable code. Sometimes the readable version is 10x slower in a hot path.

Security boundaries. Never delegated. Period.

AI tools are an amplifier. They amplify good judgment and bad judgment equally.
remixes.cta: Where do you draw the line between trusting AI output and overriding it?

This is the thing nobody talks about with AI dev tools. They compress the build cycle so much that the bottleneck shifts entirely to taste and judgment.

Now: idea --> 2 hours of building --> ship --> learn

Where AI coding tools excel: implementation of well-specified features, boilerplate generation, debugging with clear error signals, scaffolding standard patterns, and writing tests against defined behavior. These are all tasks where the "what" is already decided.

Where they consistently struggle: choosing between competing approaches, making tradeoffs that depend on future requirements, understanding organizational context that isn't in the codebase, and knowing when not to build something.

My recommendation: increase your investment in specifications proportionally to your increase in build speed. If you're building 3x faster, spend 3x more time defining what you're building and why. A precise 10-minute spec prevents an hour of rework on generated code that solved the wrong problem.

The teams getting the most value from AI coding tools are the ones treating them as implementation accelerators, not decision-making tools. The judgment layer, what to build, in what order, with what constraints, remains a human responsibility. Delegate the typing. Keep the thinking.

posts.title: The one-person GTM stack is real
posts.hook: I run lead scoring, enrichment, CRM ops, content distribution, and analytics. Solo. On a Mac Mini.
posts.body:
I run lead scoring, enrichment, CRM ops, content distribution, and analytics. Solo. On a Mac Mini.

Not because I'm trying to prove a point. Because the tools finally caught up to what one person can orchestrate.

My stack:

🔧 Clay for enrichment and scoring
🔧 Attio as the CRM (API-first, actually works)
🔧 Claude Code for building pipelines and content
🔧 Typefully for social scheduling
🔧 PostHog for analytics
🔧 Cron jobs on launchd for the glue

No Zapier. No middleware SaaS. Python scripts and API calls. When something breaks, I read the logs and fix it. When I need a new workflow, I build it in an afternoon.

Two years ago this stack would have required a RevOps person, a content marketer, and a junior dev. The tooling compression is real, but it comes with a requirement most people skip.

You have to understand every layer. Not just the UI. The data model. The API limits. The failure modes. What happens when Clay returns null on a critical field. What happens when your CRM webhook fires twice.

Solo GTM ops isn't about doing less. It's about building systems that run without you babysitting them.

The builders who get this are quietly outperforming teams of 5.
posts.cta: Anyone else running a one-person GTM stack? What's your setup?

He emphasizes the power of building in public, iterating based on user feedback, and achieving profitability through bootstrapping. His post inspires others to start small and ship fast.

remixes.label: cost-breakdown
remixes.hook: My entire GTM operation costs less per month than one SDR's base salary. Here's the actual breakdown.
remixes.body:
My entire GTM operation costs less per month than one SDR's base salary. Here's the actual breakdown.

CRM (Attio): $0. Free tier covers everything I need. API-first, actually well-designed, and I'm not paying for 200 seats I don't use.

Enrichment (Clay): This is my biggest line item. Worth every dollar because it replaces what used to require three tools and a VA.

AI inference: Under $100/month. Model routing keeps this low. Opus for content, Sonnet for workflows, local Qwen for the cron jobs.

Content distribution (Typefully): Less than one nice dinner. Handles LinkedIn and X scheduling.

Analytics (PostHog): Free tier. More than enough for tracking what matters.

Hosting: Cloudflare. Practically free at my scale.

Compute: A Mac Mini I already owned. Runs 24/7 as my cron server, dev environment, and local inference machine.

Total monthly cost: less than what most companies spend on a single tool in their stack.

This isn't about being cheap. It's about the fact that the cost floor for running real GTM operations dropped dramatically in the last 18 months. Tools got better APIs. AI handles the glue work. One person can orchestrate what used to need a team of five.

The leverage isn't the tools themselves. It's that they compose together through code instead of requiring manual workflow stitching.
remixes.cta: What's your monthly cost to run your GTM operation? Curious to compare.

remixes.label: anti-team-narrative
remixes.hook: Stop hiring for your GTM team. Start building your GTM system.
remixes.body:
Stop hiring for your GTM team. Start building your GTM system.

Every week I see startup founders posting job listings for SDR number 4, marketing coordinator number 2, RevOps analyst number 1. And every week I wonder if they've actually looked at what one person with the right stack can do in 2026.

I'm not saying fire everyone. I'm saying audit every GTM function and ask: is this a person problem or a systems problem?

Lead scoring. That's a pipeline, not a person. Enrichment. That's an API call, not a research task. CRM hygiene. That's a cron job, not an ops role. Content scheduling. That's a queue, not a coordinator.

Here's what I actually do daily with my solo setup. Morning: automated enrichment runs overnight and scores are updated. I review the top 10 accounts and decide outreach strategy. Afternoon: content pipeline generates drafts, I edit and approve. Evening: analytics dashboard shows what moved. I adjust.

The human work is strategy, relationship building, and creative judgment. Everything else is orchestration, and orchestration is what software does best.

This doesn't scale to enterprise. It doesn't need to. For startups, agencies, and solo operators, the math has changed. You can run legitimate GTM operations with one technical person and the right stack.

The question isn't whether you can afford the tools. It's whether you can afford the team when the tools already exist.

posts.title: RevOps alignment is a data problem not a people problem
posts.hook: Sales, marketing, and CS aren't misaligned because they don't talk. They're misaligned because they're looking at different data.
posts.body:
Sales, marketing, and CS aren't misaligned because they don't talk. They're misaligned because they're looking at different data.

Marketing tracks MQLs in HubSpot. Sales tracks pipeline in Salesforce. CS tracks health scores in Gainsight. Three teams. Three systems. Three versions of reality.

Then someone asks "how's that account doing?" and you get three different answers.

I've seen this pattern at every company I've worked with. The fix isn't another alignment meeting. It's a shared data layer that all three teams trust.

What that actually looks like:

1. One account record with enrichment from all three systems
2. A scoring model that weights signals from marketing, sales, AND usage data
3. A single dashboard that shows account health, not team metrics

The technical implementation isn't even that hard. Clay can normalize the enrichment. Your CRM can be the hub. A few API integrations and you're looking at the same account from every angle.

The hard part is getting three teams to agree on definitions. What's an "engaged" account? What signals matter? When does an account move from marketing to sales?

Those are data modeling decisions. Not people decisions. Treat them that way and the alignment follows.
posts.cta: What's the biggest source of data disagreement between your GTM teams?

inspired_by.author: Briana McNally
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down RevOps playbooks for aligning sales, marketing, and customer success. Highlights tools like Salesforce and HubSpot for streamlining operations. Shares metrics dashboard template for tracking revenue efficiency.

remixes.label: single-pane-of-glass
remixes.hook: Every company says they want a 'single source of truth.' Almost none of them actually build one.
remixes.body:
Every company says they want a 'single source of truth.' Almost none of them actually build one.

Instead they buy a BI tool, pipe in data from six platforms, build a dashboard, and declare the problem solved. Then a VP asks a question the dashboard doesn't answer and everyone's back in spreadsheets.

The real issue isn't the tooling. It's that nobody decided which system owns which data.

Who owns the account status? CRM says active. CS platform says at-risk. Marketing says they just opened a campaign email. All technically correct. All telling a different story.

I've started solving this by picking one system as the canonical owner for each data point. Not the best system. Not the newest system. The one that gets updated most consistently by the humans who touch it.

Then everything else syncs from that source. No voting. No averaging. One truth per field.

It's not glamorous work. Nobody posts about it on LinkedIn. But it's the difference between a revenue team that operates on shared reality and one that argues about whose numbers are right in every QBR.
remixes.cta: Which system in your stack do you actually trust when the numbers disagree?

remixes.label: meeting-tax
remixes.hook: Your weekly alignment meeting exists because your data doesn't align. Fix the data and you can cancel the meeting.
remixes.body:
Your weekly alignment meeting exists because your data doesn't align. Fix the data and you can cancel the meeting.

Think about how many hours your GTM team spends reconciling information across systems. Pipeline review where sales and marketing argue about what counts as an opportunity. CS syncs where everyone debates which accounts are actually healthy. Forecast calls where the CRM number and the spreadsheet number don't match.

These meetings aren't collaboration. They're manual data reconciliation performed by people who cost $80-200 an hour.

I tracked this at one company. Twelve hours per week across the revenue org spent in meetings that existed solely because systems didn't agree. That's over 600 hours a year. Not building pipeline. Not talking to customers. Just arguing about whose spreadsheet is right.

The fix wasn't better meetings or stronger facilitators. It was picking canonical data sources, building integrations that actually work, and killing the systems nobody maintained.

When the data agrees, the meetings get shorter. When the meetings get shorter, people actually sell things.

posts.title: I mass shipped more in public last month than in 2 years at my old job
posts.hook: In 30 days of building solo I shipped a monorepo with 3 websites, a CRM integration, a content pipeline, and an AI chat system.
posts.body:
In 30 days of building solo I shipped a monorepo with 3 websites, a CRM integration, a content pipeline, and an AI chat system.

At my last company it took 6 weeks to get a landing page approved.

I'm not saying this to trash corporate environments. Some products genuinely need that rigor. But if you're building GTM systems, developer tools, or content infrastructure... the overhead is the bottleneck, not the engineering.

What changed for me:

No PRs waiting for review from someone in a different timezone. No Jira ticket required to fix a typo. No sprint planning for a 2-hour feature. No architecture review board for a cron job.

Just git commit, verify it works, push.

The tradeoff is real though. No safety net. No one catches your mistakes. You deploy a bug at midnight, you fix it at midnight. You make a bad architectural choice, you live with it or rewrite it yourself.

But the iteration speed is a genuine competitive advantage. I can test an idea, ship it, watch the analytics, and pivot in a single afternoon. Larger teams take a quarter to do that loop once.

Building in public isn't a content strategy for me. It's an accountability mechanism. If I say I'm going to ship something, it's on the internet. That pressure is productive.

The best time to go solo is when your skills exceed what your role allows you to do.
posts.cta: What's holding you back from shipping more? Genuinely curious if it's skills, time, or organizational friction.

inspired_by.author: Sahil Lavingia
inspired_by.summary: Sahil discusses his career pivot from Pinterest to founding Gumroad as a solo builder initially, shipping the product in weeks. He reflects on growing it to millions in revenue through public updates and community feedback. The post motivates career changers entering tech entrepreneurship.

remixes.label: permission-to-ship
remixes.hook: The hardest part of going solo wasn't learning new skills. It was unlearning the habit of asking permission to ship.
remixes.body:
The hardest part of going solo wasn't learning new skills. It was unlearning the habit of asking permission to ship.

For years I'd write code, then wait. Wait for the design review. Wait for the architecture review. Wait for the PM to confirm the ticket was groomed. Wait for the sprint planning to slot it in. Wait for QA. Wait for staging. Wait for sign-off.

Now I write code, test it, and push it live. Sometimes in the same hour.

I'm not saying quality doesn't matter. I still write tests. I still think through edge cases. But I removed the 90% of the process that existed to manage coordination, not quality.

Here's what I noticed: most of that overhead existed because large teams can't trust each other's judgment at scale. When you're one person, you already have context. You don't need a meeting to share context with yourself.

The result? Three websites, a CRM integration, a content pipeline, and an AI chat system in 30 days. Not because I'm faster than a team. Because I don't have the latency of a team.

If you're building something where speed matters more than consensus, the biggest unlock might not be better tools. It might be fewer people in the approval chain.
remixes.cta: What's one thing you'd ship this week if you didn't need approval from anyone?

remixes.label: corporate-detox
remixes.hook: Two years in corporate taught me how to navigate process. One month solo taught me how much of that process was waste.
remixes.body:
Two years in corporate taught me how to navigate process. One month solo taught me how much of that process was waste.

I don't mean all process. Some of it protects users, ensures compliance, prevents outages. That stuff matters.

But a lot of what I thought was 'necessary rigor' was actually 'organizational anxiety codified into Jira tickets.'

The landing page that needed six approvals? Nobody was protecting the user. They were protecting themselves from blame if it didn't convert.

The architecture review for a simple API endpoint? Not about quality. About making sure the staff engineer felt included.

The two-week sprint cycle for a feature that took three hours to build? Not about planning. About making work legible to managers who don't read code.

When I went solo, I had to figure out which of my habits were quality practices and which were just corporate muscle memory. Turns out, about 70% of my 'process' was the second kind.

Now I ship a monorepo with three websites, CRM integrations, content automation, and AI systems. In a month. By myself.

Not because I'm 10x. Because I subtracted 10x worth of friction.
remixes.cta: What percentage of your current process actually protects quality vs. just managing organizational anxiety?

tones.builder:
In 30 days of building solo I shipped a monorepo with 3 websites, a CRM integration, a content pipeline, and an AI chat system.

At my last company it took 6 weeks to get a landing page approved.

I'm not saying this to trash corporate environments. Some products genuinely need that rigor. But if you're building GTM systems, developer tools, or content infrastructure... the overhead is the bottleneck, not the engineering.

What changed for me:

No PRs waiting for review from someone in a different timezone. No Jira ticket required to fix a typo. No sprint planning for a 2-hour feature. No architecture review board for a cron job.

Just git commit, verify it works, push.

The tradeoff is real though. No safety net. No one catches your mistakes. You deploy a bug at midnight, you fix it at midnight. You make a bad architectural choice, you live with it or rewrite it yourself.

But the iteration speed is a genuine competitive advantage. I can test an idea, ship it, watch the analytics, and pivot in a single afternoon. Larger teams take a quarter to do that loop once.

Building in public isn't a content strategy for me. It's an accountability mechanism. If I say I'm going to ship something, it's on the internet. That pressure is productive.

The best time to go solo is when your skills exceed what your role allows you to do.

First, I chose a monorepo from day one. Shared components, shared types, one deployment pipeline. No cross-team dependency negotiations.

Second, I use AI tooling aggressively. Not as a novelty, but as a multiplier for the parts of development that are repetitive. Boilerplate, integrations, content formatting.

Third, and this is the big one, I removed all process that doesn't directly improve the output. No standups. No sprint planning. No design committee reviews. Just a clear backlog, a build check, and a deploy.

If you're in a corporate environment and feeling the friction, my recommendation is this: audit your process. For every step, ask whether it improves the shipped product or just makes the organization feel safer. Then have an honest conversation about what you can cut.

Some products genuinely need heavy process. But most GTM systems, developer tools, and content infrastructure do not.

And everyone treated this as normal.

That's the part that gets me. Not that large orgs are slow. That everyone inside them has completely normalized the slowness. They've convinced themselves that shipping a landing page genuinely requires six weeks of 'cross-functional alignment.'

It doesn't. You just work at a place where launching anything requires permission from people who won't face consequences if it never launches at all.

The overhead isn't protecting quality. It's protecting careers. Every approval layer exists so someone can say 'I reviewed it' if things go wrong, and nobody gets blamed for things that never ship.

I'm building faster solo than I ever did with a team. Not because I'm better. Because nobody's incentivized to slow me down.

posts.title: Enterprise AI agents aren't replacing workers they're replacing workflows
posts.hook: The companies getting real ROI from AI agents aren't automating people. They're automating the 14-step process those people were stuck executing.
posts.body:
The companies getting real ROI from AI agents aren't automating people. They're automating the 14-step process those people were stuck executing.

I built an enrichment pipeline last month. Before the agent, the workflow looked like this:

1. Export leads from CRM
2. Manually check LinkedIn for job title accuracy
3. Cross-reference with company website for tech stack
4. Look up G2 intent signals
5. Score in a spreadsheet
6. Import back to CRM
7. Assign to rep

Seven steps. Mostly copy-paste between tabs. An SDR spent 3 hours a day on this.

Now an agent does steps 1-6 on a cron job. The SDR spends those 3 hours actually selling. Nobody got replaced. The workflow did.

This is the pattern I see working in enterprise AI adoption. Don't start with "what jobs can we automate." Start with "what multi-step workflows have humans doing robot work."

The difference matters. When you automate a workflow, the humans involved become more effective. When you try to automate a person, you get resistance, bad outputs, and a project that stalls in procurement for 8 months.

Map the workflow first. Find the robot work. Automate that. Let the humans do the judgment calls.
posts.cta: What workflow in your org has the most robot-work steps that nobody's automated yet?

inspired_by.author: Amanda Scalisi
inspired_by.summary: Analyzes the shift towards agentic AI in enterprise automation, detailing case studies where AI agents handle complex workflows like customer support and supply chain. Stresses the need for robust AI infrastructure to support multi-agent systems.

remixes.label: human-upgrade
remixes.hook: AI agents didn't replace anyone on my team. They promoted everyone by two levels of interesting work.
remixes.body:
AI agents didn't replace anyone on my team. They promoted everyone by two levels of interesting work.

Before: a sales ops person spent 4 hours daily exporting CSVs, cleaning data, cross-referencing LinkedIn profiles, and formatting spreadsheets for the account team.

After: that same person spends 30 minutes reviewing the agent's output and 3.5 hours actually analyzing accounts, building strategy, and talking to reps about what the data means.

The agent didn't take their job. It took the worst part of their job.

This pattern repeats everywhere I look. Content teams using AI to handle research and first drafts, then spending their time on strategy and originality. Finance teams automating reconciliation, then spending their time on forecasting and analysis. Support teams using AI for triage, then spending their time on complex cases that actually need human judgment.

The companies that frame AI as 'reducing headcount' are missing the bigger opportunity. The real ROI isn't doing the same work with fewer people. It's doing better work with the same people.

When you automate the robot-work, you find out what your team is actually capable of. And usually it's a lot more than data entry.
remixes.cta: What's the most tedious task on your team that, if automated, would free people up for work that actually matters?

remixes.label: workflow-autopsy
remixes.hook: I mapped out a 14-step lead enrichment workflow. Steps 2 through 12 were copy-paste between browser tabs. That's not a job. That's a script waiting to be written.
remixes.body:
I mapped out a 14-step lead enrichment workflow. Steps 2 through 12 were copy-paste between browser tabs. That's not a job. That's a script waiting to be written.

Here's what those steps actually were: export from CRM, open LinkedIn, check job title, open company website, check tech stack, open G2, check intent signals, open Apollo, verify email, open a spreadsheet, paste everything in, format the row, move to the next lead.

A human was doing this 40 times a day. Every. Single. Day.

I built an agent that chains those lookups together. It pulls from the CRM, hits the APIs for LinkedIn, company data, and intent signals, validates the results, and writes the enriched record back. The whole thing runs in about 90 seconds per lead.

But here's the part nobody talks about: building the agent wasn't the hard part. Mapping the workflow was. Most teams can't even articulate their process clearly enough to automate it. They just 'know how to do it' and the knowledge lives in muscle memory and browser bookmarks.

If you want to find your highest-ROI automation opportunity, start by making someone screen-record their most repetitive task. Then count the steps. Anything over 8 steps with minimal judgment calls is a candidate.
remixes.cta: Pick your most tedious workflow. How many of the steps actually require human judgment?

2. Manually check LinkedIn for job title accuracy
3. Cross-reference with company website for tech stack
4. Look up G2 intent signals
5. Score in a spreadsheet
6. Import back to CRM
7. Assign to rep

Seven steps. Mostly copy-paste between tabs. An SDR spent 3 hours a day on this.

This is the pattern I see working in enterprise AI adoption. Don't start with "what jobs can we automate." Start with "what multi-step workflows have humans doing robot work."

The difference matters. When you automate a workflow, the humans involved become more effective. When you try to automate a person, you get resistance, bad outputs, and a project that stalls in procurement for 8 months.

Map the workflow first. Find the robot work. Automate that. Let the humans do the judgment calls.
tones.advisor:
The companies getting real ROI from AI agents aren't automating people. They're automating the 14-step process those people were stuck executing.

I recommend thinking about AI agents through the lens of workflow decomposition, not headcount reduction. Here's the framework I use.

First, map the workflow end to end. Every click, every tab switch, every copy-paste. Most teams undercount their steps by 50% because the middle steps feel invisible.

Second, classify each step. Does it require judgment, creativity, or relationship context? Or is it lookup, validation, and data transfer? The second category is your automation target.

Third, build the agent to handle the mechanical steps and surface the judgment calls to humans. Don't try to automate the whole thing. Automate the 80% that's robot-work and let your team focus on the 20% that needs their brain.

I built an enrichment pipeline using this approach. Before: a manual process with CRM exports, LinkedIn checks, tech stack research, intent signal lookups, and spreadsheet formatting. After: an agent handles the data chain, and a human reviews the output and makes strategic decisions.

The result wasn't fewer people. It was the same people doing dramatically more valuable work.
tones.provocateur:
The companies getting real ROI from AI agents aren't automating people. They're automating the 14-step process those people were stuck executing.

But let's be honest about something uncomfortable. Those 14-step processes? They only exist because someone built a bad system and hired humans to patch over it.

Your lead enrichment workflow has 14 steps because nobody integrated the tools. Your reporting process takes two days because your data architecture is held together with VLOOKUP formulas and manual exports. Your onboarding takes three weeks because your knowledge base is a graveyard of outdated Confluence pages.

AI agents aren't solving an automation problem. They're solving a technical debt problem that companies refused to address for years because it was cheaper to hire people to do the manual work.

Now AI makes the manual work automatable for pennies. So suddenly everyone wants 'AI transformation.' But what they really want is to fix the broken workflows they should have fixed five years ago.

I'm not complaining. I build these automations for a living. But let's stop pretending we're doing something futuristic. We're doing the integration work that should have been done in 2020.

posts.title: Stop renting your audience on LinkedIn
posts.hook: LinkedIn could change its algorithm tomorrow and your 50K followers become 500 impressions.
posts.body:
LinkedIn could change its algorithm tomorrow and your 50K followers become 500 impressions.

I watched this happen on Twitter/X in real time. Creators with massive followings saw reach drop 80% overnight. No warning. No recourse. Just an algorithm update and suddenly your content doesn't exist.

So I built my own website. Blog posts, SEO, content that compounds over time on a domain I control.

The math is simple. A LinkedIn post lives for 48 hours. A blog post with decent SEO lives for years. I have posts from months ago still pulling organic traffic daily. No algorithm dependency. No platform risk.

I'm not saying quit LinkedIn. I post here every day. It's great for distribution and conversation.

But it's the top of funnel, not the foundation. The foundation is a website you own, an email list you control, and content that builds equity instead of renting attention.

My content pipeline now:

1. Write the real piece on my blog
2. Distribute clips and angles to LinkedIn, X, Reddit
3. Drive traffic back to the owned property
4. SEO does the long-term work

Every post on a rented platform should point somewhere you own. That's not marketing advice. That's infrastructure advice.

Own your stack. Own your audience. Own your distribution.
posts.cta: Do you have an owned property (blog, newsletter, website) backing your LinkedIn presence? If not, what's stopping you?

inspired_by.author: Ann Handley
inspired_by.summary: Reveals content strategy shifts for B2B GTM in 2024, focusing on video and interactive formats. Advises on repurposing content across channels to fuel outbound and inbound. Includes examples of high-ROI content campaigns.

remixes.label: landlord-analogy
remixes.hook: You wouldn't build your business on rented office space with a landlord who changes the locks whenever they want. So why are you building your audience on rented platforms?
remixes.body:
You wouldn't build your business on rented office space with a landlord who changes the locks whenever they want. So why are you building your audience on rented platforms?

LinkedIn, X, Instagram. They're distribution channels, not foundations. The algorithm decides who sees your work. The platform decides what gets promoted. You're just a tenant.

I watched creators with 100K+ followers on Twitter/X see their reach collapse 80% overnight after an algorithm change. No warning. No explanation. Just silence where engagement used to be.

So I built my own website. Not because I'm anti-social-media. I post on LinkedIn regularly. But every post I write here links back to something I own. A blog post that ranks on Google. A resource that captures an email. A page that compounds in value whether the algorithm likes me today or not.

Here's the math that convinced me. A LinkedIn post has a shelf life of about 48 hours. A blog post optimized for search can drive traffic for years. Same effort to write. Wildly different time horizon for returns.

Social platforms are megaphones. Your website is the building the megaphone points at. If you're only using the megaphone and there's nowhere for people to go, you're just making noise.

Use LinkedIn for reach. Own your website for durability.
remixes.cta: What's the oldest piece of content you've published that still drives traffic or leads today?

remixes.label: content-portfolio
remixes.hook: I treat my content like an investment portfolio. Social posts are high-risk, high-reward. Blog posts on my own site are the index fund.
remixes.body:
I treat my content like an investment portfolio. Social posts are high-risk, high-reward. Blog posts on my own site are the index fund.

Here's what I mean. A LinkedIn post might go viral and reach 50K people. Or it might reach 200. You can't predict it and you can't control it. That's the high-risk piece. Great for distribution, terrible for reliability.

A blog post on my own domain? It might start with zero traffic. But if it's optimized for search, it compounds. Month over month. Year over year. I have posts from months ago that still drive qualified traffic every single day. No algorithm required.

Most people I know in B2B are 100% allocated to the high-risk bucket. All social. No owned property. That's like putting your entire retirement in crypto and hoping for the best.

My strategy is simple. Write the long-form piece first on my blog. Then break it into social content that drives people back to the source. LinkedIn gets the hook. My website gets the depth. Google gets the long tail.

The social content promotes the owned content. The owned content builds lasting value. They work together, but only if you actually build the owned property.

A LinkedIn following without a website is a portfolio without an index fund. High energy, no foundation.
remixes.cta: What's your content allocation? What percentage lives on platforms you own vs. platforms you rent?

The owned property strategy I recommend has three layers. First, build a website with a blog. Optimize posts for search terms your audience actually searches for. This is your compounding asset. Second, build an email list. Even a small one. This is your direct line to your audience that no algorithm controls. Third, use social content to drive traffic to both.

Start with one blog post per week. Repurpose it into social content across platforms. Build the habit of creating owned content first and distributing it second. Within six months, you'll have an asset that works for you regardless of what any platform decides to do with its algorithm.

You don't own your LinkedIn audience. LinkedIn does. They let you access it for free right now because it serves their business model. The second that changes, you're done.

I watched this exact scenario play out on Twitter/X. Creators who built their entire business on platform reach woke up one morning to 80% less visibility. Some of them never recovered because they had nothing else. No website. No email list. No SEO. Just a follower count on a platform that stopped caring about them.

I built my own website specifically because I refuse to be dependent on a platform's goodwill. Blog posts that rank on Google. Content that compounds whether LinkedIn's algorithm likes me today or not.

You're not building an audience on LinkedIn. You're borrowing one. And the lender can call in the loan whenever they want.

posts.title: The real ramp time killer is your tooling not your training
posts.hook: New reps don't ramp slow because they can't sell. They ramp slow because it takes 3 weeks to learn your 11-tool stack.
posts.body:
New reps don't ramp slow because they can't sell. They ramp slow because it takes 3 weeks to learn your 11-tool stack.

Salesforce. Outreach. Gong. ZoomInfo. LinkedIn Sales Nav. Slack. Clari. 6sense. Notion. Google Sheets for the stuff that doesn't fit anywhere else. And whatever internal tool someone built in 2023 that nobody maintains.

I've onboarded onto stacks like this. The first two weeks aren't learning the product or the market. They're learning which tool has which data and which workflow lives where.

The teams I see cutting ramp time by 30-40% aren't doing better training programs. They're simplifying the stack.

Consolidate your CRM as the single source of truth. Push enrichment data TO the rep, don't make them go find it. Automate the admin work so day one is about selling, not configuring.

One team I worked with cut their stack from 9 tools to 4. Clay handled the enrichment layer. CRM handled the workflow. Sequencer handled outreach. Analytics handled measurement. That's it.

New reps were booking meetings in week one instead of week three.

If your ramp time is over 30 days, audit your tooling before you audit your training. The bottleneck is almost always operational complexity, not skill gaps.
posts.cta: How many tools does a new rep at your company need to learn before they can start selling?

inspired_by.author: Kyle Coleman
inspired_by.summary: Details automating sales ops workflows to reduce ramp time for reps by 40%. Covers integrations between CRM, dialers, and analytics tools. Warns against common pitfalls in scaling outbound automation.

remixes.label: tool-stack-audit
remixes.hook: I counted the tabs a new SDR had open on their first day of actually selling. 14 browser tabs across 9 different tools. They'd been at the company for two weeks.
remixes.body:
I counted the tabs a new SDR had open on their first day of actually selling. 14 browser tabs across 9 different tools. They'd been at the company for two weeks.

Two weeks of onboarding. Not learning the product. Not practicing the pitch. Not understanding the buyer. Just learning where to click in nine different pieces of software.

Salesforce for pipeline. Outreach for sequences. Gong for call recordings. ZoomInfo for contact data. LinkedIn Sales Nav for research. Slack for internal comms. Clari for forecasting. Google Sheets for the stuff that doesn't fit anywhere. Plus the internal tool that someone built three years ago.

Each tool has its own login, its own UI patterns, its own quirks. Some of them overlap in functionality but the team uses both because nobody ever consolidated them.

Here's what kills me. We measure ramp time in weeks and blame the rep's learning curve. But the learning curve isn't the product or the market. It's the tooling.

I've started mapping tool usage across sales orgs and the pattern is consistent. Companies with 5 or fewer core tools ramp reps in about 2 weeks. Companies with 10+ tools take 4 to 6 weeks. The extra ramp time isn't making anyone better at selling. It's just teaching them to navigate software.

Before you add another tool to your stack, ask: does this help reps sell, or does this help managers track?
remixes.cta: Open your sales team's browser right now. How many tabs are they running?

remixes.label: hidden-cost-calculator
remixes.hook: Your 11-tool sales stack costs $2,400/rep/month in licenses. But the real cost is the 3 extra weeks of ramp time multiplied by every rep you hire this year.
remixes.body:
Your 11-tool sales stack costs $2,400/rep/month in licenses. But the real cost is the 3 extra weeks of ramp time multiplied by every rep you hire this year.

Let's do the math. Say you hire 20 reps this year. Each one takes 3 extra weeks to ramp because of tooling complexity. That's 60 weeks of lost selling time. At an average AE quota of $800K, those 3 weeks represent roughly $46K per rep in delayed pipeline. Across 20 reps, that's nearly $1M in pipeline that got pushed because someone was learning Clari instead of calling prospects.

Nobody puts this number in the tool evaluation spreadsheet. The ROI calculation for every new sales tool assumes instant adoption. Zero friction. Magic onboarding.

In reality, every tool you add to the stack increases the cognitive load on every rep. And cognitive load is the silent killer of sales productivity. Not motivation. Not training. Not territory planning. Just the mental overhead of remembering which tool does what and where to find the thing they need.

I've watched the best sales leaders I know do something counterintuitive. Instead of adding tools, they audit and remove them. Consolidate three tools into one. Kill the internal app nobody maintains. Move the spreadsheet tracking into the CRM.

Subtraction is a sales enablement strategy. A surprisingly effective one.

New reps don't ramp slow because they can't sell. They ramp slow because it takes 3 weeks to learn your 11-tool stack.

Salesforce. Outreach. Gong. ZoomInfo. LinkedIn Sales Nav. Slack. Clari. 6sense. Notion. Google Sheets for the overflow. And whatever internal tool someone built in 2023.

Here's what I recommend to every sales leader who asks about ramp time. Before you redesign your onboarding program, audit your tool stack.

Start by categorizing every tool into three buckets. Essential for selling. Essential for management reporting. Used because we've always used it. Be honest about which bucket each one falls into.

Then consolidate. If two tools do overlapping things, pick one and sunset the other. If a tool exists primarily for management visibility, ask whether that visibility can come from a tool reps already use daily.

The target I recommend: five core tools maximum that a rep touches daily. Everything else should either integrate silently into those five or get removed.

I've seen this approach cut ramp time by 40% without changing anything else about the onboarding program. The product training stays the same. The pitch practice stays the same. You just remove three weeks of 'learning the software' from the equation.

Tool consolidation isn't glamorous. But it's one of the highest-ROI sales enablement investments you can make.

And here's the part nobody wants to admit. Half those tools were bought because a VP saw a demo, got excited, and signed a contract without asking the people who'd actually use the thing.

Salesforce. Outreach. Gong. ZoomInfo. Sales Nav. Slack. Clari. 6sense. Notion. Google Sheets. Plus the internal Franken-tool that engineering built two years ago and nobody wants to maintain.

You know what reps actually use? Salesforce because they have to. LinkedIn because it works. Email because it's email. Everything else is mostly theater that exists so leadership can pull reports.

But we keep adding tools. Every quarter there's a new 'essential' platform that promises to improve productivity. And every quarter, ramp time gets a little longer, context switching gets a little worse, and reps spend a little more time navigating software and a little less time talking to buyers.

The most productive sales team I've ever seen used four tools. Total. They closed more per rep than teams with triple the stack. Because their reps spent their energy selling, not toggling between browser tabs.

Your tool stack isn't enabling your team. It's taxing them. And the tax bill comes due every time you hire someone new.

platforms.newsletter:
Quick question for you: how many tools does a new rep need to learn before they make their first call at your company?

I've been thinking about this a lot because the pattern is so consistent. Companies with 10+ tools in their sales stack take 4-6 weeks to ramp new reps. Companies with 5 or fewer take about 2 weeks. The extra time isn't making anyone better at selling. It's just teaching them to navigate software.

Before you invest in better onboarding, audit your tool stack. Categorize everything as 'essential for selling' vs. 'essential for management reporting.' Then be honest about which tools fall in that second bucket and whether you actually need them.
